coredata.dc:title: Flexible frequency selective passive circuits based on memristor and capacitor
coredata.dc:creator: Ali, Shawkat
Hassan, Arshad
Hassan, Gul
Bae, Jinho
Lee, Chong Hyun
coredata.dc:description: In analog electronic communication and signal processing applications, programmable analog filters are desirable to reduce the hardware requirement. Memristor is two states configurable nonvolatile resistor, which is small size, low power, and fast switching device. To achieve tunable cut-off frequency and bandwidth, flexible memristor-capacitor (MC) circuits are proposed, which can be applied as low pass, high pass, and band pass filters. They can be fabricated on a flexible polyethylene terephthalate (PET) substrate through electrohydrodynamic (EHD) technique. In the proposed filters, two cut-off frequencies are selected through switching two resistive states of the memristor either in high resistance state (HRS) or low resistance state (LRS), whereas the capacitor has a fixed value. The memristor is composed of graphene quantum dots (GQDs)/poly 4-vinlyphenol (PVP) composite to achieve highly stable and linear behavior and the dielectric layer of capacitor is consisted of graphene/PVP composite. The fabricated MC filters are electrically and mechanically characterized, which are matched quite well with theoretical results. From these results, it can be a good basis for tunable analog filters in wearable electronics.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Memristor-capacitor filters
Inkjet printing
Flexible circuits
Programmable filters

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Theoretical results of the proposed MC filters

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagrams and layout diagrams for MC filters, (a) low pass MC filter, (b) high pass MC filter, and (c) band pass MC filter.
Different modes of the EHD as a function of applied voltage, (a) Dripping mode, (b) Unstable cone jet mode, (c) Stable cone jet mode, and (d) Multi jet mode.

Fabrication steps of the filter's components on a single substrate, (a) ITO coated PET substrate, (b) Deposition of sandwich layers of memristor and capacitor on masked ITO coated PET substrate, (c) Curing the sample, (d) Deposition of the top electrodes and unmasking, (e) Fabricated capacitor and memristor, (f) Proposed device with contact pads, (g) SEM image of memristor active layer (GQDs/PVP), and (h) cross-sectional image of memristor showing three layers of Ag/Active layer (GQDs/PVP)/ITO.

Electrical characteristics of (a) memristor I-V curve showing set/reset on ±4 V, the inset show absolute I-V curve on semi-log scale, (b) Endurance test of the memristor for 100 cycles, (c) Capacitance vs. frequency analysis of the capacitor, and (d) Capacitance Vs voltage analysis of the capacitor.

Measured results of MC filters. (a) Low pass filter response in HRS and LRS. (b) High pass filter response in HRS and LRS. (c) Band pass filter response when M1 in LRS and M2 in HRS, and M1 in HRS and M2 in LRS. (d) Band pass filter response when M1 & M2 in HRS and M1 & M2 in LRS.

Experimental setup for bending test of the proposed device under different radii such as (a) 10 mm (b) 16 mm (c) 14 mm (d) 12 mm, and (f) 8 mm. The bending results of the device over different diameters by using metallic rods having diameter from 20 mm down to 8 mm for (g) memristor and (h) capacitance.
Effect of bending on (a) HPF and (b) LPF.

Simulation results of frequency selective MC filters (a) low pass MC filter with dual cut-off frequencies f
c1 and f
c2 , (b) High pass MC filter with dual cut-off f
c1 and f
c2 , (c) MC tunable bandwidth filter, and (d) MC band selective filter.
Simulation results showing the effect of bending on (a) HPF and (b) LPF. Here, the cutoff frequency is shifted toward higher frequency due to bending.

head.ce:title: Flexible frequency selective passive circuits based on memristor and capacitor

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Ocean System Engineering, Jeju National University, 102 Jejudaehakro, Jeju 63243, South Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Electrical Engineering, National University of Computer and Emerging Sciences FAST, H 11/4, Islamabad 44000, Pakistan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National University of Computer and Emerging Sciences FAST

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In analog electronic communication and signal processing applications, programmable analog filters are desirable to reduce the hardware requirement. Memristor is two states configurable nonvolatile resistor, which is small size, low power, and fast switching device. To achieve tunable cut-off frequency and bandwidth, flexible memristor-capacitor (MC) circuits are proposed, which can be applied as low pass, high pass, and band pass filters. They can be fabricated on a flexible polyethylene terephthalate (PET) substrate through electrohydrodynamic (EHD) technique. In the proposed filters, two cut-off frequencies are selected through switching two resistive states of the memristor either in high resistance state (HRS) or low resistance state (LRS), whereas the capacitor has a fixed value. The memristor is composed of graphene quantum dots (GQDs)/poly 4-vinlyphenol (PVP) composite to achieve highly stable and linear behavior and the dielectric layer of capacitor is consisted of graphene/PVP composite. The fabricated MC filters are electrically and mechanically characterized, which are matched quite well with theoretical results. From these results, it can be a good basis for tunable analog filters in wearable electronics.

ce:section.ce:para: In modern electronic circuits, the essential demands of a circuit are flexible, small size, low power, and programmable. Among analog circuits, analog filters are the basic building blocks of electronic systems [1]. To overcome the circuit constraints of size, power, and configurability, a memristor is the best candidate for resistor capacitor RC filters. To memorize the state in memristor, if charge flows in one direction, the resistance of memristor device increases, and if charge flows in the opposite direction, resistance decreases which makes memristor a charge-controlled device that can switch between two resistive levels in analog way called high resistance state (HRS) and low resistance state (LRS) [2,3]. Once it is switched in either state, it behaves like a linear resistor under its threshold voltage levels. This resistive switching feature of the memristor can apply a non-volatile memory device as well as the replacement of a transistor. The memristor behavior was investigated mathematically in 1971 by Leon O. Chua [2], in 2008, the HP labs fabricate the first memristor and they physically demonstrate the properties of this special circuit component [3]. The resistive switching mechanism of a memristor is based on thermal, electrical, and ion-migration-induced switching [4]. Due to a simple structure and small size, memristor is being researched in many applications including resistive memory ReRAM, neuromorphic, logic circuits, and analog circuits [5–8].

Based on the memristor, various applications such as analog filters, oscillators, and chaotic circuits have been investigated by many researchers [9,10]. To fulfill the demands of smart circuits, the analog filter should have small size, dissipate low power, and easily programmable. The memristor has a small nanometer size, nonvolatile memory, and works at extreme low currents [11]. Furthermore, due to the memristor switching time is in sub-nanosecond range, all these advantages of a memristor makes it potential candidate for the configurable analog filter applications. Several researchers have been applied memristor in filtering applications, but their work is mostly based on simulation [9,10]. In simulation, memristor and capacitor are represented by their mathematical models which are stable against endurance cycles. However, in real scenario, memristor shows drift in LRS and HRS against endurance cycles, hence, the performance is not stable. To overcome this problem, a stable memristor and capacitor with a simple fabrication process are essential for printed electronics applications. In the literature [12], all inkjet printable first and second order filters have been proposed, which were consisted of resistor, inductor, and transistor. In filters design, the inkjet printed organic thin-film transistors (OTFTs) were applied [13,14] to adjust the cutoff frequency of filters ranging from 82 Hz to 740 Hz. In the proposed filters, memristor is utilized instead of transistors to increase the tuning capability of the printed device.

In this paper, we propose stable printed memristor-capacitor (MC) circuits for frequency selective low pass, high pass, and band pass filters. The cut-off frequencies are tuned by switching the memristor in either HRS or LRS state while the capacitor is keeping on a fixed value. The proposed MC filters can tune the cutoff frequency of filters ranging from several hundred kHz to several MHz e.g., LPF can adjust the frequency from 380 kHz to 4 MHz. All MC filters utilize graphene/PVP for dielectric of capacitors and GQDs/PVP for the active layer of memristor. Both components of MC filters are fabricated on the flexible PET substrate through EHD technique. These proposed filters are electrically analyzed for their frequency and phase responses by applying variable frequencies at the input, and their responses are recorded for both HRS and LRS of the memristor. The proposed MC filters are also mechanically characterized for wearable electronics application.

In this paper, MC low pass, high pass, and band pass configurations are designed and implemented. The low pass filter schematic and layout diagram is shown in Fig. 1 a, where, input signal is provided to memristor and output is taken across the capacitor. By switching two memristor states of high resistance state (HRS) and low resistance state (LRS), a cut-off frequency can be selected, whereas the value of the capacitance is fixed. When the memristor is in LRS state, it makes a combination with capacitor and provides a cut-off frequency based on the current state resistance and capacitance. As the memristor is switched to its HRS state while the capacitor is the same, the cut-off frequency changes to the lower value as the RC combination is changed. In the same way, a high pass filter is designed based on MC components as shown in Fig. 1b, where, the input is provided to capacitor and output is taken across the memristor. In this configuration, the memristor has a role to select one in two different cut-off frequencies by switching in both HRS and LRS. Band pass filter can be designed by combining two filters in cascade as shown in Fig. 1c, where, M1 and C1 are making high pass MC filter, and M2 and C2 are making low pass MC filter. The cut-off frequencies of the band pass filter also can be controlled by switching the memristors M1 and M2 in their either LRS or HRS resistive states. Here, C1 is not equal to C2 but memristors are the same.

Graphene quantum dots (purchased from sigma Aldrich) 10 mg (2–4 nm particle size) is dispersed in 1 ml ethanol, stirred on magnetic stirrer for 2 h, and it took bath sonication for 30 min. Poly(4-vinylphenol) (PVP) polymer was dispersed 10% by weight in ethanol, and stirred for 1 h on magnetic stirrer. Both inks were prepared separately, and then mixed with different ratios to obtain the optimum switching of the memristive device. The best ratio experimentally observed of GQDs and PVP was 1:0.5. Ink for the capacitor dielectric layer was prepared as: the graphene platelets (<4 layers and surface area >750 m2/g) of 0.84 mg and PVP of 0.4 g, purchased from cheap tube and sigma Aldrich, and were dispersed in NMP (4 ml) solvent by bath sonication for 30 min at room temperature. The supernatant containing in PVP/graphene was collected by centrifugation at 3000 rpm for 20 min. Silver nanoparticle (ANPs) ink for the electrodes was prepared by diluting the Ag nanopaste (PARU Corp.) by adding ethylene glycol as solvent in a 50% concentration by weight. The solution was stirred for 15 min followed by bath sonication for further 15 min.

The setting parameters of EHD system were tuned for each of the materials by adjusting applied voltage, ink flow rate, and stand-off distance. Initially, flow rate and applied voltage values were kept 50 μl/h and 0 V, and then increased gradually to observe a stable con jet mode. In the case of graphene/PVP dielectric layer deposition, ink flow rate was 280 μl/h, and the applied voltage was varied from 0 kV to 6.1 kV. By varying the voltage, different spray modes were observed including dripping mode, unstable con jet mode, stable cone jet, and multi jet mode as shown in Fig. 2 (a–d) respectively. To set up parameters, the same procedure was followed for the GQDs/PVP for memristor active layer and silver for electrodes. Details about the EHD fabrication technique can be found in our previous published work [5,15]. Prior to EHD fabrication for the devices capacitor and memristor, the PET substrate was rinsed with ethanol and then deionized water for 5 min each, and then dried to remove the contamination from the commercialized PET. After confirmation of the EHD stable cone jet, the ITO coated substrate was masked as shown in Fig. 3 and placed for the fabrication of active layer of memristor and dielectric layer of capacitor. The purpose of the substrate masking is to avoid unwanted material deposition on the substrate. The deposited both layers were deposited and cured at 130 °C for 1h. To deposit the top electrodes of the devices, mask according to the plate area of the capacitor (with 1.64 mm diameter) was placed on the capacitor dielectric layer and silver material was deposited to make the top plate of capacitor. The top electrode of the memristor was deposited by masking and then depositing the silver material through EHD system as the fabrication steps shown in Fig. 3a-d. All these fabrication steps were performed at ambient conditions. After finishing these steps, the devices were again cured at 100 °C for 30 min and the fabricated MC components are shown in Fig. 3e. The contact pads are attached with the device as shown in Fig. 3f.

The surface morphology of the MC filter components were analyzed using Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM) images as shown in Fig. 3g, which shows smooth and uniform surface on the circuit elements. For the memristor device, its cross-sectional SEM analysis was also carried out by using Jeol JSM-7600F. Here, we cut the device in cross-section and took its SEM image as shown in Fig. 3h. In the image, three smooth layers of Ag/active layer (GQDs/PVP)/ITO are clearly visible. To electrical characterize MC filters, we have used Agilent impedance analyzer 4395A (100 kHz–500 MHz) and Agilent semiconductor analyzer B5100. To study the mechanical characteristics of the proposed MC filter, we mechanically bended the device on different diameters. For this experiment, the flexible device was wrapped around metallic rods having diameter from 20 mm down to 4 mm. The experimental setup for bending test is shown in Fig. 6(a–f). The device is placed on metallic rods of different radii for bending test. The electrical characterization has been recorded through Agilent impedance analyzer. All the measurements have been carried out at ambient conditions.

The measured current of GQDs/PVP based memristor against the sweeping voltage is shown in Fig. 4 a. The memristor shows switching behavior at ±4 V and the maximum current approaches to 133 mA. The memristor switches from LRS to HRS at −4 V and vice versa at 4 V. The measured HRS and LRS of the memristor are 400 kΩ and 300 Ω respectively. The fabricated memristor has stable behavior under endurance test as shown in Fig. 4b. The average HRS and LRS value remains stable to 400 kKΩ and 300 Ω in HRS and LRS respectively against 100 endurance cycles. The response of the capacitor against frequency sweep was measured using impedance analyzer, which shows that the capacitance of the printed G/PVP based capacitor decreases with the increase on frequency. The capacitance changes from 127 pF to 90 pF under frequency sweep up to 10.1 MHz as in Fig. 4c. However, when we observed the capacitance at particular frequency 5 MHz the capacitor shows very stable behavior under sweep voltage from −3 V to 3 V with average 100 pF as shown in Fig. 4d. The responses of low pass, high pass, and band pass MC filters were measured as shown in Fig. 5 (a–c), respectively. In LPF response, the cutoff frequency increased from 380 kHz to 4 MHz when memristor switched from HRS to LRS. The cutoff frequency of the HPF was controlled through HRS and LRS of memristor. The lower and upper cutoff frequencies of HPF are 0.4 MHz–5 MHz, respectively as shown in Fig. 5b. The response of BPF shows that the bandwidth is controlled through memristor states. When the memristor M1 is in LRS and M2 in HRS the bandwidth of the BPF is wide, while the memristor switches to opposite states the bandwidth reduced as shown in Fig. 5c. In BPF, we can shift the bandwidth by using the same state of memristors in HPF and LPF stages. In HRS states of M1 and M2 memristors, the bandwidth of the BPF is shifted to lower center frequency 0.4 MHz as shown in Fig. 5d, while it shifted to 5 MHz when memristors are in LRS. Therefore, BPF can select either band depending upon the application requirement.

From the bending experimental results shown in Fig. 6g, the memristor showed normal characteristics under bending down to 8 mm diameter because both states HRS and LRS did not changed, significantly. Here, the HRS and LRS of the memristor changed from 4 kΩ to 3.525 kΩ and 300 Ω–150 Ω, respectively, under 20 mm bending diameter. However, it can be seen that the resistance of device increased under 8 mm diameter, because the silver electrodes broke down due to high mechanical stress. The capacitor was also characterized for bending test, and it showed very less changed under bending. The capacitor value did not change under bending up to 6 mm. However, when it was bent beyond 6 mm, its value is changed as shown in Fig. 6h. Under 20 mm bending diameter, it exhibits only 2% change in its capacitance as shown in zoomed image of Fig. 6h. By the bending effects on 20 mm diameter, the HP and LP MC filter devices responses are varied as shown in Fig. 7 a and b, respectively, but the proposed device is not changed, significantly.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Theoretical results of the proposed MC filters
ce:section.ce:para:
In this section, three types of MC filters, low pass, high pass, and band pass MC filters are theoretically analyzed using Matlab R2015a. In the theoretical analysis, the cut-off frequency is controlled through the resistive state of the memristor (HRS or LRS). Here, the transfer function and its cutoff frequency of low pass filter is given as following equations:
(1)
V
o u t
( s )
V
i n
( s )
=
1
M C
s +
1
M C
(2)
f c
=
1
2 π M C
where s is complex input frequency,
V
o u t
is output voltage,
V
i n
is input voltage, M is the memristive resistance, C represents capacitance, and
f c
is cutoff frequency. The memristive resistance can switch its state from HRS to LRS and vice versa. In simulation, we have selected the average values of HRS and LRS states are 4 kΩ and 300 Ω, respectively, and the value of capacitor is fixed.

Since the memristor can be switched in both HRS and LRS states, this effect changes in cut-off frequency of MC filters according to equation (2). The cutoff frequency is low when the memristor in HRS state, hence, we can control the cutoff frequency of the MC low pass filter accordingly. Fig. 8 a shows the magnitude and phase response of the low pass filter, which can be observed that the cutoff frequency is changed from low to high when the memristor switched from HRS to LRS states. Equation (3) shows the transfer function of the high pass filter. The transfer function is simulated with same HRS and LRS states values as same applied ones in low pass filter. The capacitor of 100 pF is used for high pass. Using these parameters, the response of this equation is shown in Fig. 8b.
(3)
V
o u t
( s )
V
i n
( s )
=
s
s +
1
M C

The transfer function of band pass filter (BPF) design is given in equation (4), which can be obtained by cascading a high pass filter (HPF) and a low pass filter (LPF). The memristors used in BPF can switch to either LRS or in HRS, and the HRS and the LRS values are the same that those in case of LPF and HPF, similarly capacitance of 100 pF is used for band pass MC filter design. The magnitude and phase plot of the HPF design is shown in Fig. 8b, and it can be observed that the frequency band changed with the transition of memristors from HRS to LRS or vice versa.
(4)
V
o u t
( s )
V
i n
( s )
=
s
1
M 2
C 2
s 2
+ s
(
1
M 1
C 1
+
1
M 2
C 1
+
1
M 2
C 2
)
+
1
M 1
C 1
M 2
C 2

Fig. 8a shows the magnitude and phase plot of the low pass filter. The magnitude plot shows that the cutoff frequency of filter changed when the memristor switches from LRS to HRS state or vice versa. On LRS state, the cutoff frequency is a high while in HRS state the cutoff frequency is low. The −3 dB cutoff frequency for LRS state is 5.3 MHz and in HRS, and its value is 397 kHz. The difference between upper cutoff frequency and low cutoff frequency is 4.903 MHz, which is 12.35 times of low cutoff frequency of the lowest cutoff frequency or decrease the band accordingly. The phase response of LPF shows that both graphs start from the same 0 ° phase while change of −45 ° occurs at 2.51 MHz and 33.4 MHz in HRS and LRS state respectively. HRS achieves −90 ° phase round about
2.77
×
10
9
Hz and LRS achieves at
3.66
×
10
10
Hz. As the bode plot of HPF design shown in Fig. 8b, the response shows that HPF can start at different frequencies depending upon the state of the memristor. In HRS the -3 dB response starts from 397 kHz and LRS response at 5.3 MHz. Therefore, this HPF can select its start point either 397 kHz or 5.3 MHz depending upon its HRS or LRS states respectively. Similarly, HPF can achieve 45 ° phase at 397 kHz in HRS state and at 5.3 MHz in LRS state. Hence, it has a degree of freedom of 2.

The magnitude and phase response of the band pass filter (BPF) design with controllable bandwidth is Fig. 8c. The magnitude response shows that the BPF can switch to sharp band or a wide band depending upon the memristors states, when M1 in HRS and M2 in LRS the bandwidth of BPF becomes wide (11.01 MHz) and becomes narrow (6.01 MHz) when M1 in LRS and M2 in HRS. The -3 dB bandwidth of BPF can be switched between 11.01 MHz and 6.01 MHz, and also we can tune the bandwidth by using variable capacitor. The phase plot of BPF shows that both HRS and LRS phase response starts from 90 ° and ends at −90 ° as it is second order system and shows 180 ° phase shift. The phase plot shows shifting of phase from positive to negative at 1.46 MHz. The bandwidth of BPF can be shifted from one band to another band as shown in Fig. 8d. The center frequency of BFP is 0.25 MHz when M1 and M2 are in HRS and shifted to 33.42 MHz when M1 and M2 switched to LRS. Similarly, the phase shift from positive to negative occurs at 2.5 MHz and 33.4 MHz for HRS and LRS cases, respectively.

To study the bending effect theoretically, the MC filter device was also simulated under bending as the results of LPF and HPF shown in Fig. 9 a and b, respectively. In LPF design, the cutoff frequency of the MC filter changed from 398 kHz to 406 kHz under 20 mm bending as shown in Fig. 9a when memristor was in HRS. In LRS state of memristor, the cutoff frequency of LP MC filter device changed from 5.28 MHz to 10.8 MHz under bending diameter down to 20 mm as shown in Fig. 9b. Theses response showed similar trend as the cutoff frequency shifted to higher frequency under bending in HRS and LRS. The characterizations of the MC filter devices lead toward the flexible, robust, and durable printed electronic applications.

In this paper, we have successfully demonstrated low pass, high pass, and band pass MC filters fabricated on flexible PET substrate through EHD technique. The circuit elements memristor active layer and dielectric layer of capacitor were fabricated by using GQDs/PVP and G/PVP composites, respectively. The cut-off frequencies of the filters were controlled through resistive states of the memristor (HRS/LRS), as the cut-off frequency is shifted by change in resistance value. By selecting a resistance state, the cut off frequencies can be selected between two frequencies, and the band width and frequency band of MC filters can be also tuned. The simulated results were compared with measured results, and we have achieved almost same behaviors. The proposed devices have been also characterized for bendability test and showed stable response under bending from 20 mm to 8 mm. Hence, the proposed MC filters can be applied to achieve stable response in flexible analog filter applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korea government (MSIP) (NRF-2016R1A2B4015627).
